interesting_facts: There are over 7,500 varieties of apples grown worldwide.
Apples float in water because they are 25% air.
The study of apples is called pomology.
It takes about 36 apples to make one gallon of apple cider.
Apple trees can live for more than 100 years.

interesting_facts: Bananas are technically berries botanically, while strawberries are not.
The Cavendish banana, the world's most popular variety, is threatened by a fungal disease called Panama disease.
Bananas are mildly radioactive due to their potassium-40 content.
Rubbing the inside of a banana peel on a mosquito bite can help reduce itching.
Bananas ripen faster when kept in a bunch because they release ethylene gas.

interesting_facts: Mango is the national fruit of India, Pakistan, and the Philippines.
India produces about 40% of the world's mangoes.
The mango tree can live for over 300 years and still produce fruit.
Mangoes belong to the same family as cashews and pistachios (Anacardiaceae).
Over 1,000 varieties of mango exist, including the prized Alphonso (Hapus) from India.

interesting_facts: Oranges are a hybrid of pomelo and mandarin, first cultivated in Southeast Asia around 4,000 years ago.
Brazil is the world's largest producer of oranges.
The colour orange was named after the fruit, not the other way around.
Navel oranges have a small secondary fruit embedded at their base, resembling a navel.
Orange peel contains more Vitamin C and fibre than the flesh itself.

interesting_facts: Strawberries are the only fruit that bear their seeds on the outside, with about 200 seeds per berry.
Botanically, strawberries are not true berries — they are 'accessory fruits'.
Belgium has a museum dedicated entirely to strawberries.
Strawberries were used by ancient Romans medicinally for teeth whitening and gum health.
The modern cultivated strawberry was first bred in Brittany, France, in the 1750s.

There are over 10,000 varieties of grapes grown worldwide.
Raisins are simply dried grapes and contain concentrated nutrients.
Resveratrol in red grapes is concentrated in the skin and seeds.
Grapes grow in clusters of 6 to 300 on a vine and are classified as true berries botanically.

interesting_facts: Watermelon is 91% water, making it one of the most hydrating foods available.
Watermelon originated in the Kalahari Desert of Africa, where wild varieties still grow.
The rind of a watermelon is edible and contains citrulline, an amino acid that boosts athletic performance.
In Japan, square watermelons are grown in box-shaped moulds for easier storage.
Watermelon contains more lycopene than raw tomatoes per 100g.

interesting_facts: Bromelain in pineapple is so powerful it can tenderise meat and digest the proteins in your mouth as you eat it.
A pineapple plant takes about 18–24 months to produce a single fruit.
In the 18th century, pineapples were so expensive in Europe that people rented them as a dinner table centrepiece.
Pineapple plants can only produce one fruit at a time.
Pineapple is the only known source of bromelain, a compound with significant anti-inflammatory properties.

interesting_facts: Christopher Columbus called papaya the 'fruit of the angels'.
Papain enzyme from papaya is used commercially as a meat tenderiser and in digestive supplements.
Papaya seeds are edible and have a peppery flavour; they are used as a spice and traditional deworming remedy.
India is the world's largest producer of papaya.
Unripe papaya contains high levels of latex which can trigger contractions and is avoided during pregnancy.

interesting_facts: Despite being New Zealand's national symbol, kiwi fruit originated in China, where it was called 'Yang Tao'.
Kiwi fruit contains more Vitamin C per 100g than oranges.
Eating 2 kiwis before bed has been shown in studies to improve sleep onset and duration.
The skin of the kiwi is edible and contains additional fibre and antioxidants.
Kiwi contains actinidin, a protein-digesting enzyme that makes it useful as a meat tenderiser.

The pomegranate is mentioned in ancient texts from the Egyptian, Greek, Hebrew, and Islamic traditions.
A single pomegranate contains approximately 600 arils (seeds).
Pomegranate has been used in traditional medicine for over 3,000 years.
The word 'grenade' in French originally referred to pomegranate, due to its resemblance to the shape of the explosive.

Wild blueberries (lowbush) contain twice the antioxidants of cultivated (highbush) blueberries.
North America produces about 90% of the world's blueberries.
Blueberries were consumed by Native Americans long before European colonisation.
Frozen blueberries retain almost all of their antioxidant potency, making them as healthy as fresh.

The limonin in lemons has been shown to combat cancer cells in laboratory studies.
During the 18th century, the British Royal Navy required ships to carry lemon juice to prevent scurvy.
Lemon trees originated in North-East India and were introduced to Europe via Persia and the Arab trade routes.
Lemon juice is a natural antibacterial and was historically used to purify drinking water.

interesting_facts: Peaches originated in China, where they have been cultivated for over 4,000 years.
Peaches are closely related to almonds; the peach stone (pit) contains a kernel similar to an almond.
Georgia in the USA is nicknamed 'The Peach State', though California produces most US peaches.
A medium peach contains about 285ml of water, making it excellent for hydration.
White peaches are sweeter and lower in acid than yellow peaches.

interesting_facts: Avocado is technically a berry with a single large seed.
Avocados do not ripen on the tree — they only begin to ripen after being harvested.
The Hass avocado, the world's most popular variety, was discovered by Rudolph Hass in California in 1926.
Avocado trees need both male and female flowers to produce fruit, but each flower switches between sexes.
Avocados contain more potassium per 100g than bananas.

Coconut water can be used as an emergency substitute for intravenous fluid in extreme situations.
A coconut palm can produce up to 180 coconuts per year.
Coconuts were used by Pacific Island navigators as a source of food and water on long ocean voyages.
The word 'coconut' comes from the Portuguese word 'coco', meaning skull or head.

Both the fruit and the leaves of guava are used medicinally in traditional medicine systems.
Guava leaves tea is commonly used in many countries to treat diarrhoea.
The guava tree is highly resilient and can survive drought conditions.

Turkey is the world's largest producer of cherries.
Cherry trees have short but spectacular blossoming seasons; cherry blossom festivals (Hanami) are held across Japan.
Tart cherries have been shown in studies to reduce muscle soreness after exercise by up to 22%.

interesting_facts: Unlike most fruits, pears ripen best off the tree — the texture and flavour improve after picking.
China produces over 70% of the world's pears.
The pear is one of the oldest cultivated fruits, with evidence of cultivation dating back 3,000 years in China.
There are over 3,000 known varieties of pears worldwide.
The phrase 'going pear-shaped' (meaning something went wrong) originated in the RAF, referring to a poorly flown loop manoeuvre.
